title: Grade-5 AE must be classified as serious; SAE detail block gated on AESER
description: On the AE form, when fields AESEV (severity/grade) and AESER (serious flag) both exist: AESER gets constraint `(${AESEV}!='5') or (.='Y')` and constraint_message 'Grade 5 AE must be classified as serious.' Any SAE detail block on the form gets relevant `${AESER}='Y'` so SAE-only fields show only for serious AEs.
rationale: Grade-5 AEs are fatal by CTCAE definition. Marking a Grade-5 AE as non-serious is always an error. The constraint catches this at entry. The SAE detail block (date of death, hospitalization details, narrative) is irrelevant when AESER='N' and showing it noisily implies it must be filled in even when blank — gating it on AESER='Y' keeps the form focused.

ensure.field.constraint_message: Grade 5 AE must be classified as serious.

flag.message: AESER constraint pinned to Grade-5-must-be-serious rule. Verify any SAE detail block on form ${form.form_id} has relevant `${AESER}='Y'` so SAE-specific fields only appear when serious='Y'.

effect.soft: After applying the AESER constraint, locate any SAE-detail group/block on the form (typically labelled 'SAE Details', 'Serious Adverse Event Information', or similar) and set its top-level relevant attribute to `${AESER}='Y'`. The SAE-block detection is form-specific and not yet expressible in the DSL.

history.note: Imported from prompts.py RULE OC-7F during Phase B.1c-final. Uses field.has_sibling from B.1c-1 to verify AESEV-AESER pairing. The constraint expression on AESER is fixed (references the known sibling name AESEV, not a derived prefix), so ensure can emit it directly — partially structured. The SAE-block relevance assignment remains soft because the SAE block has no canonical identifier; identifying it requires form-walking the engine can't do today. Note: the plan doc originally suggested default_value composition (AESEV='Severe' → AESER defaults to 'Y'); on closer reading prompts.py uses a constraint (the value must reconcile), not a runtime default. The actual rule is a hard constraint, not a soft default. default_value is not used in this convention; it remains documented as the operator-of-record for future runtime-default rules.
